data.title: Bullish sentiment falls sharply while bearish views rise, AAII survey shows
data.description:
Investor sentiment deteriorated sharply, with bearish views surging and optimism fading, according to the latest survey conducted by the American Association of Individual Investors (AAII).

The American Association of Individual Investors said in its survey that the bullish camp plunged to 31.4% for the week ended July 1, from 44.9% in the prior week. Meanwhile, bearish sentiment rose to 42.3%, compared to 36.1% last week.

Ongoing market volatility in the global markets due to the turmoil between Iran and the U.S. as well as the intense selling pressure in the tech sector has kept investors on their toes.

According to AAII, neutral sentiment, or expectations that stock prices will stay essentially unchanged over the next six months, rose to 26.4%, compared to 18.9% last week.

The survey has been conducted by the American Association of Individual Investors since 1987, in which it asks respondents for their thoughts on where the market is heading in the next six months.

S&P 500 Tracking Funds: (MUTF:FXAIX), (MUTF:VFIAX), (MUTF:VFFSX), (MUTF:SWPPX), (NYSEARCA:SPY), (NYSEARCA:VOO), (NYSEARCA:IVV), (NYSEARCA:RSP), (NYSEARCA:SSO), (NYSEARCA:SH), (NYSEARCA:SDS), and (NYSEARCA:SPXU).

data.title: 7-Eleven sues Nike over Air Max 95 stripe design
data.description:
7-Eleven has sued Nike (NKE), alleging the sportswear giant's upcoming Air Max 95 sneaker infringes its trademark by using a stripe design resembling the convenience store chain's signature orange, green, and red branding.

The lawsuit, filed on Wednesday in federal court in Texas, says the shoe is set for release on July 11, a date associated with 7-Eleven Day and the retailer's annual Free Slurpee Day promotion. It alleges the design could lead consumers to mistakenly believe the shoe is sponsored or endorsed by 7-Eleven.

The retailer said it has used the tricolor design for decades and owns multiple trademark registrations covering it. It also alleged it unsuccessfully sought to resolve the dispute before filing suit.

7-Eleven is seeking to block sales of the shoe, recall products already distributed, and recover monetary damages and Nike's profits from the footwear.

data.title: Tesla beats Q2 delivery estimates -- update
data.description:
Updated 12:55 ET with share price movement

  • Tesla (TSLA) delivered 480,126 vehicles in the second quarter, beating estimates of 406K deliveries and briefly driving shares higher into Thursday’s open.
  • The company produced 451,758 units.
  • In the second quarter, Tesla (TSLA) also said it deployed 13.5 GWh of energy storage products.
  • After matching Wednesday's high and achieving four days in the green, Tesla (TSLA) shares turned negative as profit-taking accelerated, leaving the stock down 8% on the day.

data.title: Jersey Mike's “shores” up interest in public offering
data.description:
Jersey Mike’s (JMKE) is moving closer to becoming a publicly traded company as the sandwich chain filed a registration statement with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission for an initial public offering.  

The company plans to list on the NYSE under the ticker JMKE with two classes of stock, both of which entitle the holder to one vote per share.

The size of the offering has not yet been disclosed.

Jersey Mike’s started out in 1956 as “Mike’s Subs,” a small sub shop in Point Pleasant, New Jersey, named after owner Michael “Mike” Ingravalo. The shop was purchased in 1975 by 17-year-old employee Peter Cancro with $125K borrowed from his high school football coach.  

Recognizing the attractive franchising opportunities demonstrated by the habits of Jersey Shore tourists who quickly became repeat customers, Cancro began franchising the newly renamed “Jersey Mike’s.”

The chain’s expansion to 3,300 locations across the U.S. and Canada caught the attention of private equity giant Blackstone, which acquired a majority stake in January 2025 in a deal valuing the company at $8B. Under the terms of the deal, Cancro retained a 10% stake and stayed on as CEO and chairman, with Blackstone's “Submarine Buyer” LLC owning the remaining 90%. While Cancro remains involved in the company's operations, Charlie Morrison, the former CEO of Wingstop (WING), was recently selected as Jersey Mike's new CEO.     

"Our company stands out because of our incredible franchise owners and team members—with our Blackstone partnership and Charlie's leadership, we are well positioned for the next chapter of growth," Cancro said in a letter to shareholders.

data.title: Midday Need to Know: Payrolls miss, Apple's new hardware release & more
data.description:
Wall Street’s major indexes were mixed on Thursday due to a weaker-than-expected June non-farm payrolls report that lowered expectations for the Federal Reserve to hike interest rates and a tech trade that continues to struggle.

Now, here are four news stories that broke in the morning to watch out for:

Nonfarm payrolls miss expectations: U.S. job growth came in at just 57,000 in June, sharply below the 114,000 consensus estimate and down from 129,000 in May, which was itself revised lower from an initial print of 172,000. The unemployment rate unexpectedly declined to 4.2% from 4.3% as the labor force participation rate slipped to 61.5%. Average hourly earnings held steady at +0.3% month-over-month, suggesting wages aren't fueling inflation. The soft print has reduced trader bets on a rate hike at this month's FOMC meeting, with the probability of a 25-basis-point increase falling to 17.6% from 28.9% the prior day. 

Trump administration proposes drug pricing changes: The Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services  announced plans to revise payment rates to hospitals participating in the 340B Drug Pricing Program starting next year. The proposal aims to align payment rates for 340B drugs with documented acquisition costs, potentially cutting federal drug spending by roughly $5.7 billion. The VanEck Vectors Pharmaceutical ETF rose approximately 3% to its highest level since March, with AbbVie reaching a 52-week high.

Apple schedules new hardware releases: Apple (AAPL) is  preparing to introduce a redesigned 14-inch MacBook Pro and next-generation iPad Pro tablets in the first half of 2027, according to Bloomberg. The company is testing four iPad Pro models with existing 11-inch and 13-inch displays, with improvements focused on internal changes including faster chips. The new MacBook Pro will feature a design similar to higher-end MacBooks with touch screens. The announcement comes after Apple raised prices on most products last month due to ongoing memory shortages, with analysts warning the increases could create "demand friction" for both product lines.

data.title: Hassett seems to confirm Trump administration is talking to AI firms about a possible stake
data.description:
National Economic Council Director Kevin Hassett seemed to confirm that OpenAI (OPENAI) has proposed giving the U.S. government a 5% stake in the artificial intelligence company.

“We’re talking to all the big AI companies and seeing what they think about what they could do to share their upside with the American people,” Hassett said in a Thursday interview with CNBC. Hassett did not give further details on what the discussions entailed. 

However, Hassett said it was an “open question” of whether the U.S. federal government would take a stake in companies, similar to what  it did with Intel (INTC). He described the Intel stake as being driven by “unique” circumstances.

The Financial Times reported that OpenAI has discussed giving a 5% stake to the U.S. federal government as the $852B AI start-up seeks to clear political obstacles. OpenAI CEO Sam Altman has argued that giving the public a financial stake in the company is the best way to share the upside of AI. 

Altman has been in active talks with the Trump administration about the issue of public ownership, including President Trump, Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick, and Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent, according to multiple people familiar with the matter.

The proposed arrangement would involve other U.S. AI companies handing over a similar stake, although it is not clear if the other labs would be willing to do so, the report said.

Separately, on Thursday, Reuters reported that Anthropic (ANTHRO) has not spoken to the U.S. government about taking a financial stake in the Claude maker.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

(This story has been updated with news on Anthropic.)

data.title: BHP seeks permit for $1.5B restart of Cerro Colorado copper mine
data.description:
BHP (BHP) said late Wednesday it filed an environmental permit application for a $1.5B project to reopen the Cerro Colorado copper mine in Chile for an additional 20 years.

BHP (BHP) said it plans to use treated wastewater transported through a ~60-mile pipeline from the municipality of Alto Hospicio to the mine site.

The company closed the mine in 2023 but said it would explore options to restart operations, including through the use of leaching technologies and desalinated water.

Separately, BHP (BHP) said Thursday it agreed to sell its San Manuel property in Arizona to Faraday Copper (CPPKF) in exchange for shares equivalent to a 30% fully diluted interest in Faraday, raising its stake in the company to 32.5%.

The agreement provides a pathway for the development of a new copper hub in Arizona, combining existing infrastructure and mineral inventory at San Manuel with Faraday’s (CPPKF) adjacent Copper Creek project.

data.title: JPMorgan Chase teams up with Gulf banks on $7B loan for Syrian projects - report
data.description:
JPMorgan Chase (JPM) is joining a group of Gulf lenders to arrange about $7B of debt financing for a Qatari firm's projects in Syria, one of the biggest foreign commitments to the country's reconstruction, according to a media report on Thursday.

The Wall Street bank is working with Qatar National Bank and Abu Dhabi Commercial Bank PJSC on the loan for projects being worked on by a consortium that includes Power International Holding, one of Doha's largest conglomerates, Bloomberg reported, citing people familiar with the matter.

The loan will help finance the reconstruction of power infrastructure and an airport, the people said. The five-year loan will be guaranteed by QNB, which has the Qatar Investment Authority as its largest shareholder, the article said.

data.title: Crinetics wins new Buy at UBS after recent pullback
data.description:
Crinetics Pharmaceuticals (CRNX) traded higher on Thursday after UBS launched its coverage with a Buy recommendation and a 12-month target of $55, citing an attractive entry point following the stock’s recent decline.

While shares of the San Diego, California-based pharma company have lost more than 10% this year, analyst Ashwani Verma sees opportunity ahead, with multiple catalysts lined up for the next 12-18 months.

Crinetics’ (CRNX) once-daily, oral ACTH antagonist, atumelnant, which is currently undergoing development for congenital adrenal hyperplasia and ACTH-dependent Cushing’s syndrome, is among Verma's most promising bets.

“We believe investors are not giving full credit to atumelnant's pursuit in CAH and/or ACTH-Dependent Cushing's Syndrome,” he wrote.

With a 70% probability of success, the analyst projects $2.0B in risk-adjusted peak sales for atumelnant in CAH alone. Across the company’s four programs, Verma estimates $4.2B in risk-adjusted peak sales compared with $3.0B implied by the stock’s current trading level.

data.title: Mortgage rates hit seven-week low, housing activity estimated to bolster through summer
data.description:
Mortgage rates reached a seven-week low, and housing activity is expected to strengthen through the summer.

30-year fixed-rate mortgages averaged 6.43% as of July 2, down from 6.49% last week and 6.67% in the same period a year ago, according to the latest Freddie Mac Primary Mortgage Survey.

15-year fixed-rate mortgages averaged 5.79%, down from 5.84% a week ago and 5.80% a year ago.

"With rates at a seven-week low and purchase demand continuing to edge higher, it's an encouraging sign as prospective homebuyers respond to modest improvements in affordability," said Sam Khater, Freddie Mac's chief economist.

"Last week's modest decline in mortgage rates helped sustain borrower interest, with home purchase demand up slightly and continuing to outpace last year's levels. Buyers are benefiting from a more balanced housing market as inventory improves and home-price growth moderates in many areas," said Mortgage Bankers Association's CEO Bob Broeksmit.

"If these trends continue, they should bolster housing activity through the summer," said Broeksmit.
